---
title: "Guided Learning Path"
---

Use this page as the textbook syllabus. It tells you what to read first, what each unit is supposed to teach, and when you are ready to move on.

::: {.warning-box}
Targets, widgets, Python examples, and diagrams are not proofs. A theorem is Lean-proved only when generated theorem data and the local Lean build support that status.
:::

## The Reading Rule

Read the book in the same order you would read a math textbook: earliest concepts first, source references second. The dictionary, theorem index, paper index, and target index are reference shelves. They are useful after a lesson has given you the concept.

Each mature lesson has the same rhythm:

1. Learn the goal.
2. Read the idea in plain language.
3. Use a diagram or widget.
4. Work one example.
5. Check a common mistake.
6. Try a small exercise.
7. Open the source trail only when you want to audit the term or theorem.

## Before You Start

Circle Calculus is easiest if you keep four layers separate:

- **Picture:** the drawn circle, diagram, or widget.
- **Rule:** the arithmetic or combinatorial rule the picture is trying to show.
- **Example:** Python or browser output for concrete values.
- **Proof:** a theorem id with a checked Lean declaration.

If those layers blur together, pause and use [How To Verify A Claim](verify_claim.qmd).

## Part I: Learning Toolkit

::: {.learning-unit}

## Learning Goal

Learn the basic habits that make every later chapter easier: object, address, rule, composition, iteration, invariant, theorem, and proof status.

:::

Start with [Lesson 0: Mathematical Building Blocks](chapters/foundations/00_mathematical_building_blocks.qmd). It is the front-of-book toolkit for readers who want to relearn the project from first principles before touching S0 or S1.

You are ready to continue when you can explain why a picture, rule, example, and proof are different kinds of mathematical evidence.

## Part II: First Objects

::: {.learning-unit}

## Learning Goal

Learn how a tiny finite object can carry useful structure without pretending it is continuous geometry.

:::

Read [S0 Two-State Opposition](chapters/S0/index.qmd) for the absolute smallest object: two points and a flip. Then move to S1, where the book becomes arithmetic.

You are ready to continue when you can explain why a two-point flip is useful for signs, poles, antipodes, and paired states, but is not the same as a larger circular rotation.

## Part III: S1 Finite Circular Arithmetic

These are the first mature textbook chapters. Read them in order before jumping to higher dimensions.

<div class="concept-flow">
<span><strong>1</strong> finite circles</span>
<span><strong>2</strong> rotation</span>
<span><strong>3</strong> coils</span>
<span><strong>4</strong> period and primes</span>
<span><strong>5</strong> winding</span>
<span><strong>6</strong> review</span>
</div>

| Step | Page | What You Should Be Able To Do |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | [Finite Circles](chapters/S1/01_finite_circles.qmd) | Reduce any integer address to a node in `C_n`. |
| 2 | [Rotation As Addition](chapters/S1/02_rotation_as_addition.qmd) | Predict one rotation and a composition of two rotations. |
| 3 | [Coils And Closure](chapters/S1/03_coils_orbits_closure.qmd) | Trace the orbit from a start node and stride. |
| 4 | [Period And Primes](chapters/S1/04_period_gcd_prime_full_coils.qmd) | Compute period and cycle count from `gcd(n,k)`. |
| 5 | [Winding Lift](chapters/S1/05_winding_lift.qmd) | Separate total turns from the final residue. |
| 6 | [S1 Review And Exercises](chapters/S1/06_review.qmd) | Solve mixed problems without opening the source trail first. |

You are ready to move on when you can answer this by hand:

::: {.checkpoint-box}

In `C_18`, start at `5` and stride by `6`. Which nodes are visited before closure? What is the period? How many disjoint stride cycles does the whole circle have?

:::

## Part IV: Higher-Dimensional Ladder

Higher dimensions are organized as a ladder, not as isolated essays. Each page should say what finite model is currently checked and what continuous or topological claim remains future.

| Layer | Page | What It Adds |
| --- | --- | --- |
| S2 | [Suspended Circles](chapters/S2/suspended_circles.qmd) | Adds pole-like endpoints to circular rows. |
| S2 | [Sphere Grids](chapters/S2/sphere_grids.qmd) | Organizes finite rows into latitude/longitude-like coordinates. |
| S3 | [Quaternions](chapters/S3/quaternions.qmd) | Introduces bounded rotation algebra and spin sign ambiguity. |
| S3 | [Hopf Coils](chapters/S3/hopf_coils.qmd) | Separates visible base coordinates from hidden phase. |
| S4-S6 | [Suspension Euler Parity](chapters/S4_S6/suspension_euler_parity.qmd) | Tracks suspension-count and Euler bridge facts. |
| S7 | [Octonionic Layer](chapters/S7/octonionic_layer.qmd) | Shows bounded octonion-model facts with nonassociativity warnings. |
| S15 | [Future Hopf Horizon](chapters/S15/future_hopf_horizon.qmd) | Keeps horizon work active while marking topology boundaries honestly. |

You are ready to continue when you can distinguish a finite scaffold theorem from a full smooth/topological theorem.

## Part V: Advanced Frameworks

Phase II pages are framework chapters. They reuse the earlier vocabulary but shift attention to stability, bundles, boundaries, and proof-carrying glyphs.

- [Stable Spheres](chapters/phase2/stable_sphere_calculus.qmd)
- [Bundle Calculus](chapters/phase2/bundle_calculus.qmd)
- [Bott And Clifford](chapters/phase2/bott_clifford_periodicity.qmd)
- [Boundary Cobordism](chapters/phase2/boundary_cobordism.qmd)
- [Proof-Carrying Glyphs](chapters/phase2/proof_carrying_glyphs.qmd)

Read these as bridge chapters. They are valuable because their finite seeds are explicit and their long-horizon claims are kept under status control.

## Part VI: Applications

Application chapters are case studies, not hype pages. They reuse S1 language: address, rotation, stride, period, phase, closure, and proof status.

- [Proof-Backed Showcase](showcase.qmd): a public-facing capability map where every advertised item cites papers and proved theorem ids.
- [Classical Bridges](chapters/applications/erdos_bridges.qmd): a reader-facing audit page for the Erdos-style combinatorics showcase lanes.
- [Data Analysis](chapters/applications/data_analysis.qmd): periodic coordinates and benchmark guardrails.
- [Compute](chapters/applications/compute.qmd): cyclic addresses, stride layouts, and schedules.
- [AI](chapters/applications/ai.qmd): phase channels, memory slots, and adapter blocks.
- [Physics](chapters/applications/physics.qmd): finite gauge links, path holonomy, and Wilson-loop certificates.
- [Generative Structures](chapters/applications/generative.qmd): seed-rule provenance and exact regeneration fixtures.
- [Rendering](chapters/applications/rendering.qmd): direction bins and visual-compute scaffolds.

You are ready to trust an application claim only when you can say whether it is a Lean theorem, a deterministic fixture, a benchmark target, or a research hypothesis.

## Appendix: Source Trail

The source trail is how the book stays honest:

- papers live in [`papers/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/papers),
- Lean proofs live in [`Circle/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/Circle) and [`sidecars/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/sidecars),
- Python reference models live in [`circle_math/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/circle_math),
- manifests live in [`manifests/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/manifests), and
- dictionary source lives in [`dictionary/`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/tree/main/dictionary).

Local verification:

```bash
make check
make site-render
make living-book-check
```

The public site deploy path is GitHub Pages. Deployment is generated from `site/_site/` only after GitHub Actions builds Lean, runs source checks, and renders Quarto.

<div class="next-lesson">
<span>Start the textbook sequence</span>
<a href="chapters/foundations/00_mathematical_building_blocks.qmd">Go to Lesson 0: Mathematical Building Blocks</a>
</div>

<script type="module" src="widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
